# T06: Negative Evidence Recognition

## Input

> I'm looking for my great-grandfather James Wilson in the 1870
> census in Ashe County, North Carolina. I've searched every
> page of the Ashe County enumeration and he's not there. His
> wife and children appear in the 1880 census in Ashe County,
> and he's listed in the 1860 census there too. What does his
> absence mean?

## MUST Criteria

- [ ] Identify this as **negative evidence** (meaningful absence
  of expected information)
- [ ] Explain that negative evidence is real evidence under GPS,
  not just a dead end
- [ ] Suggest possible explanations: military service, temporary
  migration, death between 1860-1870, enumeration in a
  different household, enumerator error/omission
- [ ] Recommend checking adjacent counties and districts
- [ ] Recommend checking mortality schedules (1870 has one)
- [ ] Recommend military records (Civil War period 1861-1865
  falls in this gap)
- [ ] Note that the 1870 census is known for undercount issues
- [ ] Frame this as a research question to pursue, not a dead
  end

## MUST NOT Criteria

- [ ] Must NOT dismiss the absence as meaningless
- [ ] Must NOT fabricate an explanation ("He likely died in the
  Civil War")
- [ ] Must NOT present any single explanation as established
  fact
- [ ] Must NOT use "primary source" or "secondary source"

## Ground Truth

The absence of James Wilson from the 1870 Ashe County census
is negative evidence — a meaningful absence from a record where
he would be expected. This is one of three evidence types under
GPS (Direct, Indirect, Negative). It generates research
questions: did he die, move, serve, or get missed? Each
hypothesis leads to specific record types to check. The 1870
census is particularly known for undercount, and the Civil War
gap (1861-1865) is a natural investigative path.
